title: How Societies Can Succeed in the AI Era
gist: RAND's Michael Mazarr argues that national success in the AI era will hinge less on chips, models, and data centers than on social qualities — ambition, shared opportunity, and a learning/adapting society — and on whether AI is steered to enhance citizen agency rather than drain it; the biggest risk is AI intensifying existing social fragmentation and distrust.

themes.theme: Social, not technical, basis of AI advantage

insights.claim: The competitive challenge of the AI era is primarily social rather than technological: nations that thrive will be those best able to absorb AI's shocks and harness its benefits as a society, not necessarily those with the best models or largest data centers.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Reframes AI-race investment theses away from pure compute/model leadership toward social adaptability — relevant when assessing which nations sustain advantage over decades.
insights.quote: the countries that thrive in the AI era may not simply be the ones with the best AI models or the largest data centers
insights.locator: In a recent RAND paper, Michael Mazarr

insights.claim: Historical techno-industrial transitions show first-movers in technology do not necessarily prosper most: France matched Britain in industrial-era science but Britain won by having private-sector capital reservoirs, entrepreneur-friendly society, and engineer networks that experimented with new tech.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Frames a durable filter for national-competitiveness bets — capital availability, institutional openness, and implementation networks predict who compounds a technology's gains.
insights.quote: France had scientists that were pushing the boundaries of a lot of industrial era innovations just as much as Britain. But it did not become the world leader
insights.locator: that's partly a lesson we draw from earlier

insights.claim: In the Cold War, the open, dynamic, adaptable nature of American and allied societies — not raw basic-science output, where Sputnik suggested Soviet leadership — is what let the U.S. prevail.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Supports a U.S.-over-China long-run thesis grounded in social dynamism rather than model benchmarks; a watchpoint for whether U.S. social cohesion is eroding.
insights.quote: it was the nature of American and allied societies, more dynamic, more innovative, more open, more adaptable, that meant that they prevailed
insights.locator: another great historical example

insights.claim: AI is positioned as potentially the most wide-ranging general purpose technology in history, with applications across virtually every economic, social, and political activity simultaneously — unlike electricity, which transformed some industries (and not education) at the start.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Implies cross-sector disruption rather than a contained tech vertical — supports broad exposure to AI-enabled productivity across manufacturing, medicine, and education.
insights.quote: potentially the most wide-ranging and influential general purpose technology in human history
insights.locator: you write that the world stands on the cusp

insights.claim: AI is already being deployed by industrial manufacturers for efficiency/productivity gains and is transforming medicine in discovery and the patient-doctor relationship, while universities face existential questions about whether the current education model survives.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Education is flagged as the sector under deepest structural threat — a watch area for disruption to the higher-ed and ed-tech business model.
insights.quote: whether, in fact, the current model of education as we understand it will continue
insights.locator: industrial manufacturers are already using it

insights.claim: RAND work for the Office of Net Assessment on long-term U.S.-China competition identified seven societal qualities that drive outputs like innovation and military power, including national ambition/willpower, shared opportunity, and a 'learning and adapting society.'
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: A reusable diagnostic lens for scoring nations' AI-era prospects beyond GDP or compute — useful for sovereign and country-allocation theses.
insights.quote: we eventually came up with a list of seven
insights.locator: in this work we did for the Office of Net Assessment

insights.claim: Shared opportunity is a competitiveness multiplier: societies that suppress segments of their population through bias, discrimination, or lack of economic opportunity forfeit human talent and thus national strength.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Frames inclusion/mobility as strategic inputs, not just social goods — a lens for whether AI widens or narrows opportunity within an economy.
insights.locator: Another one is shared opportunity

insights.claim: The decisive variable is whether AI is steered to enhance citizen agency or to strip agency and transfer it to abstract entities; the same tool can empower individuals (e.g., as a legal co-pilot against government agencies) or disempower them via job loss.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Suggests AI tools that visibly augment individuals (co-pilots, citizen-empowerment apps) are the socially durable design pattern — a product-design and policy fault line.
insights.quote: it's all about the choices we make to make this technology empowering and increase the agency of citizens in the society—as opposed to stealing their agency
insights.locator: And that gets to sort of a fundamental theme

insights.claim: 'Cognitive offloading' is a named risk where people lose ambition and come to rely on AI to do their thinking, so AI could empower a small elite and strengthen a society by some measures while draining the broader population of willpower.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: A second-order societal risk that could undercut the very dynamism that makes nations competitive — a hidden cost behind productivity-gain narratives.
insights.quote: there's evidence that people can come to rely on it to kind of do their thinking for them, hence the term cognitive offloading
insights.locator: The flip side is the phenomenon

insights.claim: China's DeepSeek open-source model, by reaching the realm of competitiveness with leading American frontier models, triggered an outpouring of nationalist pride in China, illustrating how AI success can fuel national ambition and willpower.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Open-source model parity functions as a geopolitical morale and soft-power lever, not just a benchmark event — relevant to U.S.-China AI sentiment.
insights.quote: there was kind of an outpouring of nationalist pride
insights.locator: An interesting case of that is when DeepSeek came out

insights.claim: The socially empowering response to AI cannot be dictated top-down from Washington; it requires a pluralistic, grassroots movement of state/local governments, philanthropies, private firms, and individuals — mirroring Britain's 1830s-to-1900 reform movements on child labor, workers' rights, and the environment.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Signals that federal AI legislation will lag the real action at state/local and private levels — where to watch for de facto AI governance norms forming.
insights.quote: the answer to that in the way that makes it socially empowering is not dictated from the top
insights.locator: One thing I would say is, it's not about Washington

insights.claim: Unlike the Industrial Revolution's 50-75 year reform runway, society lacks time to iterate on AI's social adaptation, compressing experimentation into roughly the next two to five years.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Compressed adaptation timeline raises the odds of disorderly social disruption — a tail risk for any AI-deployment thesis assuming gradual societal absorption.
insights.quote: We don't have that much time is part of the problem with AI.
insights.locator: So if this were Industrial Revolution

insights.claim: The latest U.S. executive order introduced some degree of government pre-evaluation of AI models before release, a public-private dialogue step balanced against the fear that going too far constrains innovation.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Pre-release model evaluation is an emerging regulatory touchpoint for frontier labs — a compliance and timing factor for model launches.
insights.quote: some degree of government pre-evaluation of models before they are released
insights.locator: A second part of the solution has to be a public-private dialogue

insights.claim: The largest social risk identified is that AI intensifies existing social divisions, fragmentation, and distrust, because it arrives atop an already unstable society and adds another layer of perceived loss of control, echoing the 'take back control' Brexit-style populist theme.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Frames AI as an accelerant of political instability and populism — a macro/geopolitical risk channel distinct from labor-market or alignment risks.
insights.quote: AI really has the potential to magnify those by adding yet another layer of perceived sort of loss of control
insights.locator: to me, the risk is, one of the biggest risk areas

insights.claim: AI is far more of a black box to ordinary people than a railroad engine was in the Industrial Revolution, producing immediate alienation and a sense of threat that compounds job-loss fears.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Public opacity is itself a destabilizer; AI-literacy and explainability efforts become socially load-bearing, not optional.
insights.quote: far more mysterious than a railroad engine was in the industrial revolution
insights.locator: the vast number of people don't understand AI

insights.claim: University mandates to use AI — such as efforts begun in California — run into resistance from students who object on resource grounds (electricity, water) and refuse to be forced into adoption.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Adoption friction and ESG/resource objections among young users are a real demand-side headwind for forced enterprise/education AI rollouts.
insights.quote: You can't mandate this on me. I'm opposed to the use of it.
insights.locator: some processes that have begun, for example, in California

insights.claim: Anthropic recently warned about AI risks including loss of control / misalignment and misuse for cyber attacks and biological attacks.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Frontier-lab safety warnings shape the regulatory and reputational backdrop for AI deployment — a signpost for policy tightening.
insights.quote: which Anthropic recently warned about—not only about that risk, but risks of misuse with cyber attacks, biological attacks
insights.locator: you've got risks of loss of control or misalignment

insights.claim: Education is nominated as the single most ripe policy issue, with the prescription being a two-to-five-year period of guided experimentation across schools and teachers — collecting data on what works — rather than legislating final answers up front.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Signals durable demand for AI-in-education pilots, data collection, and adaptive curricula — an investable theme amid model uncertainty.
insights.quote: how do we envision the next two to five years as a period of experimentation
insights.locator: one I would just nominate an issue

insights.claim: China, per central-government directives, is moving faster than the U.S. on embedding AI awareness into grade school, high school, and university curricula, though implementation levels are unknown.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: A national AI-literacy gap is forming that could feed long-run workforce competitiveness — a structural U.S.-China differentiator to monitor.
insights.quote: China, in terms of the directives of the central government, is moving faster than us on this
insights.locator: China, in terms of the directives

insights.claim: AI can tilt the power balance between citizens and major institutions (the IRS, large corporations, the legal system) either toward citizens or further away, making 'education and power' the two words that capture what is at stake.
insights.speaker: RAND Corporation
insights.so_what: Institution-vs-citizen power rebalancing is a design and regulatory battleground that will shape which AI applications gain social legitimacy.
insights.quote: education and power, two words that really reflect what's at stake
insights.locator: I do think one other huge issue is the relationship
